title: Aviation Noise and Emissions Mitigation Act
short_title: Aviation Noise and Emissions Mitigation Act
plain_summary: The Aviation Noise and Emissions Mitigation Act creates two programs to help communities that are bothered by airplane noise and air pollution from nearby airports. The first program gives money to universities and researchers to study and measure how loud airplanes are and how much pollution they create. The second program gives money to community groups and local governments to find ways to protect people from airplane noise and dirty air, especially in neighborhoods that need the most help. Both programs are run by the Environmental Protection Agency and focus on helping people who live near airports.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Aviation Noise and Emissions Mitigation Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Noise and Air Quality Monitoring and Research Grant Program
sections.text:
Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the head of the Environmental Protection Agency (the government agency that protects the environment) must work with the Transportation Secretary and other government agencies to create a three year test program. This program will give grants (money awards) to qualified groups to measure airplane noise and pollution in communities near airports or where planes fly overhead. The groups must use advanced technology that can trace noise and pollution back to specific sources like airplanes and airports. They must be able to identify which neighborhoods, buildings, or areas are affected by this noise and pollution.

The groups that get grants must do several things. They must use technology that creates data showing where noise and pollution come from so communities can understand what causes their problems. They must try to create a program that can be copied in other places across the country for ongoing data collection near airports and flight paths. They must create detailed information for specific neighborhoods and ZIP codes to determine how much noise and pollution affects certain areas and to identify communities that are hit hardest by these problems. They must coordinate and regularly talk with local clean air agencies, local public health departments, local planning organizations, tribal governments, community groups, and other local, state, or nonprofit organizations that can help with the project. They must work with community groups to help create a plan to reduce the impact of airplane and airport noise and pollution on affected communities. They must establish a clear plan for sharing the data and results of the program regularly with the public. They must talk with the public and relevant community groups or local governments from the beginning and throughout the process about how to design and run the noise and pollution monitoring program.

Colleges, universities, other nonprofit research groups, health institutions, or local governments with proven experience in studying airplane noise or pollution can apply for these grants. The Environmental Protection Agency head can give out no more than six grants. Each grant can be between two million five hundred thousand dollars and five million dollars for the three year period. Each group that gets a grant must submit a yearly report to the Environmental Protection Agency head about their research and findings. The Environmental Protection Agency head must make these reports available to the public.

sections.title: Section 3  Mitigation and Support Services Grant Program
sections.text:
Within six months after the final yearly report is submitted from the first grant program, the head of the Environmental Protection Agency must work with the Department of Health and Human Services, Department of Transportation, and other relevant government agencies to create a test grant program. This program will help reduce airplane and airport noise and pollution and their effects in communities near airports and flight paths.

The Environmental Protection Agency head must give priority and provide guidance and technical help to applications that focus on communities found to be most severely affected by airplane noise or pollution from the first grant program research, primarily help disadvantaged communities and communities with higher rates of diseases linked to environmental exposures, and show community support and involvement from affected communities in their proposed program.

To be eligible for a grant, an applicant must be a local community nonprofit organization, a group of community nonprofit organizations from a community that participated in the noise and pollution monitoring program from the first section, a local public health department, or a local or tribal government. If the recipient is a public health department or local or tribal government, they must show community support for the program and how they plan to partner with at least one nonprofit community organization.

Applicants must show in their application how the research from the first section informs their proposed project design, how their proposed project addresses environmental or health inequalities and the needs and concerns of affected communities including how they used mapping tools to target solutions effectively, how they incorporated input from affected communities and established ongoing engagement plans, and how they intend to partner with relevant local organizations and government entities like public health departments, tribal governments, community health centers, and local education authorities.

Grant money must be used to reduce the effects of airplane or airport noise and pollution on communities near airports or flight paths. This can include noise reduction packages with weatherization, retrofitting, or energy efficiency upgrades that reduce noise and provide environmental or health benefits for households, schools, or other facilities while prioritizing low income households and under resourced communities. It can include programs to promote environmental and public health services in impacted communities including programs that address the unequal effects of climate change on environmental justice communities. It can include health care services and environmental and public health interventions that address underlying impacts from airport noise and pollution on health and quality of life especially in children, vulnerable populations, disadvantaged communities, and communities of color. Each grant must last at least three years and no more than five years as determined appropriate by the Environmental Protection Agency head.

One year after awarding grants and every year after that, the Environmental Protection Agency head must submit a report to specific congressional committees about the grant program activities. The report must include the services provided and who they helped, the steps taken by grant recipients to engage the public and impacted communities, a breakdown of the areas served and how they align with locations identified in the first section, and general information about the communities, households, and individuals benefiting from the services including demographic and economic information.

Three years after awarding the first grant, the agency must submit a report to the same congressional committees. This report must include an assessment of whether the grant program successfully reduced airport and aviation noise and pollution, potential lessons learned to inform future efforts to address noise and pollution impacts in communities near airports and flight paths, and a review of how noise and pollution levels and impacts from airports and flight paths can be incorporated into government environmental justice mapping tools. The report must be completed with input from grant recipients and affected communities.
